humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark uses historical Czech archival documents (content) and is designed specifically to trace social, political, and historical themes in digital humanities and archival research (domain).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Monolingual historical Czech texts extracted via PERO-OCR from digitized historical books and periodicals.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates document-level identification of semantic topic presence and the precise word-level extraction/localization of text spans matching abstract, human-defined topic descriptions, requiring nuanced semantic understanding and context interpretation.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed as a domain-focused benchmark for topic localization in historical Czech archival documents rather than general-purpose NLP evaluation.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-5 variants, GPT-OSS, Llama 3, Gemma 3, and Gemini 3 Pro.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems evaluated and compared against human annotators and fine-tuned BERT baselines.

arts_humanities_data.note: Focuses on Language and Linguistics (Czech language parsing, OCR text) and History (historical archives, social and political historical themes).

quantitative_metrics.note: Precision, Recall, F1 score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and Krippendorff's alpha for inter-annotator agreement.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, though the evaluated dataset consists of historical Czech texts.

summary: This paper introduces CzechTopic, a human-annotated benchmark for topic localization in historical Czech texts. Models must identify and localize word-level spans corresponding to human-defined, open-ended topic descriptions, evaluating semantic mapping capabilities on fuzzy and subjective thematic boundaries. The study establishes human baselines through inter-annotator agreement and benchmarks diverse LLMs alongside specialized BERT-based cross-encoders.
key_findings: Large language models show a large performance gap, with word-level F1 scores spanning from 13.2 to 61.1. The best model (GPT-5-2) approaches but does not surpass human agreement (61.1 vs. 68.7 F1). Specialized fine-tuned BERT models (e.g., RobeCzech) remain highly competitive with larger LLMs, achieving an F1 of 48.3. Methodologically, matching-based span extraction outperforms tagging-based extraction by 10.4 F1 points, while prompt translation (Czech vs. English) has no significant effect.
